string_basics.label: String Basics: Working With Text
string_basics.summary: Learn how strings work, how to format output, how indexing and slicing read text, and how methods like `lower()`, `strip()`, and `replace()` clean it.

project.title: Project: Clean text and build output

fstring_greeting.title: Use an f-string for a clean sentence

strings_intro.title: What Strings Are
strings_intro.bodyMarkdown:
You already learned how Python stores and uses values:

- variables store values
- data types describe what kind of values those are
- operators and expressions compute results

Now we focus on one type you will use constantly:

**Strings** (`str`) — text.

Strings store things like names, messages, emails, passwords, search terms, and anything the user types with `input()`.

One rule is especially important:

> `input()` always returns a string.

So when you understand strings, you understand a big part of how programs read and show text.

## What is a string?

A string is text inside quotes.

~~~python
name = "Maya"
city = 'Chicago'
~~~

Single quotes and double quotes both work. It is usually best to pick one style and stay consistent.

printing_strings.title: Printing Text and Variables
printing_strings.bodyMarkdown:
## Showing text with `print()`

You have already used `print(...)` to show output in the terminal.

Now you can use it to display text and variables together.

## Concatenation

Concatenation means joining strings together with `+`.

~~~python
first = "Maya"
last = "Johnson"
full = first + " " + last
print(full)                  # prints: Maya Johnson
~~~

This works well, but both sides of `+` must be strings.

~~~python
age = 16
print("age: " + age)         # TypeError because age is an integer
~~~

That causes a `TypeError`.

You can fix it by converting the number first:

~~~python
age = 16
print("age: " + str(age))    # prints: age: 16
~~~

## Commas in `print()`

Commas are often simpler:

~~~python
age = 16
print("age:", age)           # prints: age: 16
~~~

Python prints each value with a space between them.

## F-strings

F-strings are usually the cleanest way to mix text and variables.

~~~python
name = "Maya"
age = 16
print(f"Hi {name}, you are {age} years old.")  # prints: Hi Maya, you are 16 years old.
~~~

They read almost like a normal sentence.

indexing_and_slicing.title: Indexing and Slicing
indexing_and_slicing.bodyMarkdown:
## Strings are sequences of characters

A string is made of characters in order.

Each character has a position called an **index**.

Indexes start at `0`.

~~~python
word = "Python"
print(word[0])               # prints: P
print(word[1])               # prints: y
~~~

Python also supports negative indexes.

`-1` means the last character.

~~~python
word = "Python"
print(word[-1])              # prints: n
print(word[-2])              # prints: o
~~~

## Slicing

Slicing lets you take part of a string.

Format: `text[start:end]`

- it includes `start`
- it stops before `end`

~~~python
word = "Python"
print(word[0:2])             # prints: Py
print(word[2:6])             # prints: thon
~~~

Shortcuts also work:

~~~python
word = "Python"
print(word[:2])              # prints: Py
print(word[2:])              # prints: thon
~~~

Indexing reads one character.
Slicing reads a section of the string.

string_methods.title: String Methods and Clean Input
string_methods.bodyMarkdown:
## Common string methods

Methods are built-in tools attached to strings.

### `lower()`
Converts text to lowercase.

~~~python
msg = "HeLLo!"
print(msg.lower())           # prints: hello!
~~~

### `strip()`
Removes extra spaces at the beginning and end.

~~~python
raw = "   hello   "
print(raw.strip())           # prints: hello
~~~

### `replace()`
Replaces one piece of text with another.

~~~python
text = "I like cats"
print(text.replace("cats", "dogs"))  # prints: I like dogs
~~~

## Cleaning user input

User input is often messy.

~~~python
name = input("Enter your name: ")
clean = name.strip().lower()

print("raw:", name)         # if input is '  Maya  ', prints: raw:   Maya  
print("clean:", clean)      # prints: clean: maya
print(f"Hello, {clean}!")   # prints: Hello, maya!
~~~

## A small username generator

~~~python
first = input("First name: ").strip()
last = input("Last name: ").strip()
username = (first[0] + last).lower()
print(f"username: {username}")  # if inputs are Maya and Johnson, prints: username: mjohnson
~~~

That combines strings, indexing, methods, and formatted output.

## What this lets you do

Now you can:

- display messages clearly
- combine text with variables
- access characters inside text
- extract part of a string
- clean user input

m1_str_concat_vs_comma_sc.title: Concatenation vs commas
m1_str_concat_vs_comma_sc.prompt: Assume `age = 16`. Which line prints **without** an error?
m1_str_concat_vs_comma_sc.hint: Commas work with numbers. Using `+` requires strings on both sides.

help.concept: Using commas in `print` lets Python display mixed values easily, but `+` requires both sides to be strings.
help.hint_1: Look for the option that avoids joining a string directly to a number with `+`.
help.hint_2: The safe line uses a comma inside `print`.

m1_str_fstring_placeholder_sc.title: f-string placeholder
m1_str_fstring_placeholder_sc.prompt: Which line correctly inserts the variable `name` into an f-string?
m1_str_fstring_placeholder_sc.hint: Inside an f-string, put the variable name inside curly braces.

help.concept: F-strings insert variable values using curly braces inside a string that starts with `f`.
help.hint_1: The line must start with `f` and use `{name}`.

m1_str_strip_sc.title: What strip() does
m1_str_strip_sc.prompt: What does `.strip()` do to a string?
m1_str_strip_sc.hint: It removes extra spaces from the beginning and end.

help.concept: `strip()` cleans surrounding whitespace from text.
help.hint_1: It does not lowercase the text.
help.hint_2: It removes spaces at the start and end.

options.a: It removes spaces from the beginning and end.
options.c: It removes every letter from the string.

m1_str_lower_sc.title: What lower() does
m1_str_lower_sc.prompt: What does `.lower()` do to a string?
m1_str_lower_sc.hint: It changes uppercase letters into lowercase letters.

help.concept: `lower()` converts letters to lowercase.
help.hint_1: It changes case, not spacing.

options.b: It keeps only the first letter.
options.c: It converts the string to lowercase.

m1_str_username_steps_mc.title: Username-building steps
m1_str_username_steps_mc.prompt: Select all steps used to build the username correctly.
m1_str_username_steps_mc.hint: The username uses cleaned text, the first letter of the first name, and lowercase output.

help.concept: The username pattern in the lesson uses cleaning, indexing, and lowercase conversion.
help.hint_1: You do not use the full first name.
help.hint_2: The correct steps are strip, first character, and lowercase.

options.c: Take the first character of the first name.

m1_str_concat_truths_mc.title: String-building truths
m1_str_concat_truths_mc.prompt: Select all true statements about strings in this lesson.

help.concept: Strings can be combined in several ways, but each technique has rules.
help.hint_1: Commas in `print()` are flexible.
help.hint_2: Using `+` works when both sides are strings.

options.a: Using commas in `print()` can show strings and numbers together.
options.b: Using `+` with text works when both sides are strings.
options.d: `.lower()` turns a string into a number.

m1_str_indexing_first_char_mc.title: First character with indexing
m1_str_indexing_first_char_mc.hint: `[0]` means the first character in the string.

help.concept: Indexing accesses characters by position, and index `0` means the first character.
help.hint_1: It does not mean the whole string.

options.d: You can use `first[0]` when building a username.

m1_str_fill_string_definition.title: What makes a string
m1_str_fill_string_definition.prompt: Fill in the blank with the best word.

help.concept: Strings are written as text surrounded by quotes.
help.hint_1: Single and double quotes both work.

m1_str_fill_first_index.title: First index
m1_str_fill_first_index.prompt: Complete the sentence with the best answer.
m1_str_fill_first_index.template: The first character in a string has index ____.

help.concept: Python sequences start at index `0`.
help.hint_1: The first position is not `1`.

m1_str_fill_last_index.title: Last character index
m1_str_fill_last_index.prompt: Fill in the blank with the best answer.
m1_str_fill_last_index.template: The last character in a string can be accessed with index ____.
m1_str_fill_last_index.hint: Negative indexing counts from the end.

help.concept: Negative indexes count backward from the end of the string.
help.hint_1: The final character is not at index `0` unless the string has one character.

m1_str_fill_lower_meaning.title: Meaning of lower()
m1_str_fill_lower_meaning.prompt: Complete the sentence with the best word.

help.concept: `lower()` changes letters into lowercase form.
help.hint_1: It changes case, not type.

m1_str_fill_strip_meaning.title: Meaning of strip()
m1_str_fill_strip_meaning.prompt: Fill in the blank with the best word.
m1_str_fill_strip_meaning.template: The `.strip()` method removes extra ____ at the beginning and end.
m1_str_fill_strip_meaning.hint: Think about messy input like `   Maya   `.

help.concept: `strip()` removes whitespace around text.
help.hint_1: This helps clean user input.

help.concept: The username builder cleans input, combines characters, then normalizes case.
help.hint_1: Do not lowercase before creating the base username.

m1_str_reorder_fstring_idea.title: Order the f-string idea
m1_str_reorder_fstring_idea.prompt: Reorder the parts into the lesson idea.

help.concept: F-strings make it easy to place variables inside readable text.
help.hint_1: The sentence starts with f-strings.

m1_str_reorder_clean_input.title: Order the input-cleaning flow
m1_str_reorder_clean_input.prompt: Reorder the steps for cleaning a user name.

help.concept: Messy input is read first, then cleaned with methods.
help.hint_1: You cannot strip before reading input.

m1_str_reorder_indexing_idea.title: Order the indexing idea
m1_str_reorder_indexing_idea.prompt: Reorder the basic idea of indexing.
m1_str_reorder_indexing_idea.hint: The idea moves from the string to its positions.

help.concept: Indexing works because strings are ordered sequences of characters.
help.hint_1: Start with the big idea about strings.
help.hint_2: Then move to indexes and the first position.

m1_str_reorder_print_options.title: Order the output tools
m1_str_reorder_print_options.prompt: Reorder these string-output ideas from simpler to cleaner.
m1_str_reorder_print_options.hint: The lesson moves toward the cleanest modern style.

help.concept: The lesson shows several ways to combine strings and values, ending with the cleanest modern style.
help.hint_1: F-strings come last.

m1_str_concat_vs_comma_code.title: Concatenation vs commas
m1_str_concat_vs_comma_code.prompt:
Read TWO inputs:
1) name
2) age

Print EXACTLY:
Hello <name>
age: <age>
m1_str_concat_vs_comma_code.hint: Use commas in `print()` or convert age with `str(...)`.

help.concept: This checks whether you can safely print text together with a number.
help.hint_1: Read the name as text and the age as an integer.
help.hint_2: Print the greeting line, then print the age line without causing a type error.

m1_str_fstring_greeting_code.title: f-string greeting
m1_str_fstring_greeting_code.prompt:
Read ONE input (name).

Print EXACTLY:
Hello, <name>!

help.concept: This checks whether you can use a simple f-string with one variable.
help.hint_1: Store the input in `name`.
help.hint_2: Use an f-string with `{name}` inside the text.

m1_str_username_code.title: Username generator
m1_str_username_code.prompt:
Read TWO inputs (first, last).

Rules:
- strip spaces
- username = first letter of first + last
- lowercase
Print ONLY the username.

help.concept: This combines stripping, indexing, lowercase conversion, and final output.
help.hint_1: Clean both inputs first.
help.hint_2: Take the first character of the first name, join it with the last name, lowercase the result, then print it.
